docs.title: Definitely Not Java
docs.text: Java is cool.

docs.title: Software Programs
docs.text:
• A program is a collection of data variables and instructions
  • Programs are written in programming languages using keywords, symbols, and names
  • Programming languages provide higher level abstraction for complex instructions

docs.title: Program Execution Methods
docs.text:
• Two main approaches to execute programs:
    1. Compilation: Translates entire program to machine code before execution
    2. Interpretation: Reads and executes program statements one at a time

docs.title: Java Execution
docs.text:
• Java uses a hybrid approach: "Write once, run anywhere" principle
  • Two ways to run Java programs:
    1. Compile to bytecode first, then interpret/compile via JVM
    2. Direct interpretation using Java interpreter
  • Java Virtual Machine (JVM):
    • Platform-specific runtime environment
    • Converts bytecode to machine code using Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation
    • Handles memory management and garbage collection
    • Provides platform independence through abstraction layer

docs.title: Compilation Process
docs.text:
• Java compiler (javac) compiles source code (.java) to bytecode (.class)
  • Basic compilation steps: 
    javac Hello.java    # creates Hello.class
java Hello          # runs the program
  • Compilation phases:
    1. Parsing: Source code to syntax tree
    2. Semantic analysis: Type checking and symbol resolution
    3. Bytecode generation: Platform-independent instructions
  • Common mistakes to avoid:
    • Confusing javac and java
    • Launch Single-File Source-Code Programs before compiling. E.g. java Hello.java
  • Classpath considerations:
    • Default is current directory
    • Set with -cp or -classpath option
    • Controls where Java looks for classes

docs.title: Java Shell (jshell)
docs.text:
• Interactive tool for Java code evaluation (introduced in Java 9)
  • Allows direct code testing without compilation (REPL environment)
  • Useful for learning and experimentation
  • Can run in two modes:
    • Interactive mode: jshell at command prompt
    • Script mode with .jsh files: jshell filename.jsh
  • Basic jshell commands:
    • /list: Show entered code snippets
    • /exit: Exit the shell
    • /help: Display help information
    • /imports: List imported packages

docs.title: Compiler Functions
docs.text:
• Translates source code to machine code/bytecode
  • Checks code for syntax errors
  • Performs type checking
  • Can be either:
    • Conservative: Reports error if possibility of incorrect statement
    • Permissive: Only reports error if statement is definitely incorrect

docs.title: Compiler Limitations
docs.text:
• Cannot detect all runtime errors during compilation
  • Cannot always determine if code will be executed
  • Cannot always determine variable values at compile time

docs.title: Software Programs
docs.text:
• A program is a collection of data variables and instructions
  • Programs are written in programming languages using keywords, symbols, and names
  • Programming languages provide higher level abstraction for complex instructions

docs.title: Java Execution
docs.text:
• Java uses a hybrid approach: "Write once, run anywhere" principle
  • Two ways to run Java programs:
    1. Compile to bytecode first, then interpret/compile via JVM
    2. Direct interpretation using Java interpreter
  • Java Virtual Machine (JVM):
    • Platform-specific runtime environment
    • Converts bytecode to machine code using Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation
    • Handles memory management and garbage collection
    • Provides platform independence through abstraction layer

docs.title: Compilation Process
docs.text:
• Java compiler (javac) compiles source code (.java) to bytecode (.class)
  • Basic compilation steps: 
    javac Hello.java    # creates Hello.class
java Hello          # runs the program
  • Compilation phases:
    1. Parsing: Source code to syntax tree
    2. Semantic analysis: Type checking and symbol resolution
    3. Bytecode generation: Platform-independent instructions

docs.title: Data Abstraction: Variable
docs.text:
• Variables are named abstractions for accessing values stored in memory
  • Primitive variables store actual values and are independent
  • Reference variables store memory addresses and can share objects

docs.title: Java Type System
docs.text:
• Static typing: Variables must declare type, checked at compile time
  • Strong typing: Enforces strict type rules, prevents unsafe conversions
  • Types help manage complexity and enable compile-time checking

docs.title: Java Primitive Types
docs.text:
• Boolean: boolean (1 bit)
  • Character: char (16 bits)
  • Integral Types: byte (8 bits), short (16 bits), int (32 bits), long (64 bits)
  • Floating-Point: float (32 bits), double (64 bits)

docs.title: Type Relationships
docs.text:
• Subtyping defines when one type can substitute for another (T <: S)
  • Primitive conversions: byte <: short <: int <: long <: float <: double
  • Memory considerations affect primitive type selection

docs.title: Computation Abstraction
docs.text:
• Functions group instructions under a name
  • Allow composition at higher levels of abstraction
  • Java functions (methods) require return type declaration

docs.title: Benefits of Functions
docs.text:
• Compartmentalization: Isolates computation and effects
  • Implementation hiding: Separates what from how
  • Code reuse: Reduces repetition through parameterization
  • Abstraction barrier: Separates callers from implementers

docs.title: Function Types
docs.text:
• Pure functions: Same input always gives same output, no side effects
  • Non-pure functions: May modify external state or depend on it
  • Variable arguments: Methods accepting variable number of parameters

docs.title: Class and Object Concept
docs.text:
• Class: Bundles composite data type with associated functions
  • Components:
    • Fields (states, attributes, or properties)
    • Methods (functions that operate on the fields)
  • Objects: Instances of a class
  • Example: 
    class Circle {
  private double x;
  private double y;
  private double r;

  double getArea() {
    return Math.PI * r * r;
  }
}

docs.title: Reference Types in Java
docs.text:
• Classes are reference types
  • Variables store references to objects, not objects themselves
  • Multiple variables can reference same object
  • Special reference value: null indicates no object reference
  • NullPointerException occurs when attempting to use null reference

docs.title: Access Modifiers
docs.text:
• Control access to class members:
    • private: Only accessible within the class
    • public: Accessible from anywhere
  • Access control enforced by compiler
  • Example: 
    class Circle {
  private double x;  // hidden from outside
  private double y;
  private double r;

  public double getArea() {  // accessible from outside
    return Math.PI * r * r;
  }
}

docs.title: Abstraction Barrier
docs.text:
• Direct access to internal fields breaks abstraction
  • Makes code dependent on implementation details
  • Changes to implementation can break client code
  • Reduces code maintainability

docs.title: Constructors
docs.text:
• Special methods to initialize objects
  • Same name as class
  • No return type
  • Called automatically with new
  • Example: 
    class Circle {
  private double x;
  private double y;
  private double r;

  public Circle(double x, double y, double r) {
    this.x = x;
    this.y = y;
    this.r = r;
  }
}

docs.title: Accessor and Mutator Methods
docs.text:
• Accessor (getter): Methods to retrieve field values
  • Mutator (setter): Methods to modify field values
  • Benefits over public fields:
    • Adds layer of abstraction
    • Can add validation in mutators
    • Can control access to fields

docs.title: Principle Explained
docs.text:
• Tell objects what to do
  • Don't ask for internal state
  • Let objects perform their own operations
  • Example: 
    // Don't do this:
double cX = c.getX();
double cY = c.getY();
double r = c.getR();
boolean isInCircle = ((x - cX) * (x - cX) + (y - cY) * (y - cY)) <= r * r;

// Do this instead:
boolean isInCircle = c.contains(x, y);

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text:
• Maintains encapsulation
  • Reduces coupling between classes
  • Makes code more maintainable
  • Keeps related computations together
  • Allows implementation changes without affecting clients

docs.title: Class Field Concept
docs.text:
• Fields associated with class rather than instances
  • Declared using static keyword
  • Shared across all instances of the class
  • Can be accessed without creating class instance
  • Example: 
    class Math {
  public static final double PI = 3.141592653589793;
}

docs.title: Common Modifiers for Class Fields
docs.text:
• static: Makes field belong to class
  • final: Makes field value unchangeable
  • public: Makes field accessible outside class
  • Common combination: public static final for constants

docs.title: Instance vs. Class Fields
docs.text:
• Instance fields:

    • Belong to specific object instances
    • Each instance has its own copy
    • Accessed through object reference

  • Class fields:

    • Belong to class itself
    • Single copy shared by all instances
    • Accessed through class name
    • Exist even if no instances created

docs.title: Class Method Concept
docs.text:
• Methods associated with class rather than instances
  • Declared using static keyword
  • Can be called without creating class instance
  • Cannot access instance fields/methods directly
  • Cannot use this keyword
  • Example: 
    class Math {
  public static double sqrt(double x) {
    // compute square root
  }
}

docs.title: Static vs Non-Static Methods
docs.text:
• Static methods:

    • Belong to class
    • Can only access static members
    • Called through class name
    • No access to this

  • Non-static methods:

    • Belong to instances
    • Can access all members
    • Called through instance
    • Has access to this

docs.title: The main Method
docs.text:
• Special static method as program entry point
  • Required signature: 
    public static void main(String[] args)

docs.title: Factory Methods
docs.text:
• Static methods that create objects
  • Benefits:
  • Better naming than constructors
  • Can return cached instances
  • Can return different subtypes

  • Control object creation

  • Example: 

    public class Color {
  private final int red;
  private final int green;
  private final int blue;

  private Color(int r, int g, int b) {
    red = r;
    green = g;
    blue = b;
  }

  public static Color of(int r, int g, int b) {
    // Validation and caching logic here
    return new Color(r, g, b);
  }
}

docs.title: JVM Memory Regions
docs.text:
• Method area: Stores method code
  • Heap: Stores dynamically allocated objects
  • Stack: Stores local variables and call frames

docs.title: Object Lifecycle
docs.text:
• Objects created with new on the heap
  • References stored on stack or as fields in other objects
  • Objects persist across method calls
  • Garbage collector reclaims unreferenced objects
  • Example: 
    void method() {
  Circle c = new Circle(0, 0, 5);  // c on stack, Circle on heap
  doSomething(c);                  // reference passed
}  // c goes out of scope, Circle may be garbage collected

docs.title: Inheritance Concept
docs.text:
• Mechanism to extend existing code
  • Models "IS-A" relationship between classes
  • Uses extends keyword in Java
  • Example: 
    class ColoredCircle extends Circle {
  private Color color;
}

docs.title: Key Characteristics
docs.text:
• Subclass inherits:
    • Public fields from parent
    • Public methods from parent
  • Private members remain inaccessible
  • super keyword accesses parent members
  • Example: 
    class ColoredCircle extends Circle {
  public ColoredCircle(Point center, double radius, Color color) {
    super(center, radius);  // call parent constructor
    this.color = color;
  }
}

docs.title: When to Use Inheritance
docs.text:
• Use when subclass IS-A superclass
  • Subclass should be substitutable for superclass
  • Example of proper use: 
    // Square IS-A Shape
class Square extends Shape { }

docs.title: Object Class
docs.text:
• Every class implicitly inherits from Object
  • Object provides common methods:
    • equals(Object obj): Check equality
    • toString(): String representation
    • hashCode(): Hash value

docs.title: Method Overriding Rules
docs.text:
• Method must have:
    • Same name
    • Same parameters (number, type, order)
    • Same return type (or covariant)
    • Same or less restrictive access
  • Cannot override:
    • final methods
    • static methods
    • private methods

docs.title: @Override Annotation
docs.text:
• Indicates method is meant to override
  • Helps catch errors at compile time
  • Best practice to always use it
  • Example: 
    @Override  // Compiler checks if parent has this method
public boolean equals(Object obj) {
  // implementation
}

docs.title: Method Overloading Concept
docs.text:
• Multiple methods with same name but different parameters
  • Differs from overriding (which uses same parameters)
  • Based on method signature differences
  • Example: 
    class Calculator {
  public int add(int x, int y) { return x + y; }
  public int add(int x, int y, int z) { return x + y + z; }
  public double add(double x, double y) { return x + y; }
}

docs.title: Method Signature Components
docs.text:
• Method name
  • Parameter types
  • Parameter order
  • Number of parameters
  • Does NOT include:
    • Return type
    • Parameter names
    • Access modifiers

docs.title: Polymorphism Concept
docs.text:
• Ability of objects to take multiple forms
  • Method behavior depends on object's runtime type
  • Enables code reuse and flexibility
  • Example: 
    Shape s = new Circle(p, 1.0);  // Circle behaves as a Shape
s.getArea();  // calls Circle's getArea method

docs.title: Dynamic Binding
docs.text:
• Method calls resolved at runtime
  • Based on actual object type, not variable type
  • Also called late binding
  • Example: 
    void say(Object obj) {
  System.out.println(obj.toString());  // calls appropriate toString
}

docs.title: Type Casting
docs.text:
• Converting between supertype and subtype
  • Widening conversion (subtype to supertype):
    • Automatic
    • Always safe 
      Circle c = new Circle(p, 1.0);
Shape s = c;  // widening, no cast needed
  • Narrowing conversion (supertype to subtype):
    • Requires explicit cast
    • May cause runtime errors 
      Shape s = new Circle(p, 1.0);
Circle c = (Circle) s;  // narrowing, needs cast

docs.title: LSP Definition
docs.text:
• Subtype must be completely substitutable for its supertype
  • Subtype must preserve behavior expected of supertype
  • A subclass should pass all test cases of its superclass

docs.title: Preventing Inheritance
docs.text:
• Use final keyword to prevent inheritance
  • Example: 
    final class String {  // Cannot be inherited
  // implementation
}

docs.title: Preventing Method Overriding
docs.text:
• Use final keyword on methods
  • Prevents subclasses from changing behavior
  • Example: 
    class Circle {
  final double getArea() {  // Cannot be overridden
    return Math.PI * r * r;
  }
}

docs.title: Abstract Class Concept
docs.text:
• Class that cannot be instantiated
  • May contain abstract and concrete methods
  • Declared using abstract keyword
  • Example: 
    abstract class Shape {
  abstract double getArea();  // abstract method

  boolean isSymmetric() {     // concrete method
    return true;
  }
}

docs.title: Abstract Methods
docs.text:
• Methods without implementation
  • Must be implemented by concrete subclasses
  • Declared using abstract keyword
  • Cannot be private or final
  • Example: 
    abstract class Shape {
  abstract double getArea();  // subclasses must implement
}

docs.title: Concrete Classes
docs.text:
• Classes that implement all abstract methods
  • Can be instantiated
  • Must override all inherited abstract methods
  • Example: 
    class Circle extends Shape {
  private double radius;

  @Override
  double getArea() {  // implements abstract method
    return Math.PI * radius * radius;
  }
}

docs.title: When to Use Abstract Classes
docs.text:
• Define common characteristics of related classes
  • Share code among several related classes
  • Want to declare non-public members
  • Need to provide default behavior

docs.title: Interface Concept
docs.text:
• Contract specifying behavior
  • Collection of abstract methods
  • Can have constants (public static final)
  • Declared using interface keyword
  • Example: 
    interface GetAreable {
  double getArea();  // implicitly public abstract
}

docs.title: Implementing Interfaces
docs.text:
• Classes use implements keyword
  • Can implement multiple interfaces
  • Must implement all methods
  • Example: 
    class Circle implements GetAreable {
  @Override
  public double getArea() {
    return Math.PI * radius * radius;
  }
}

docs.title: Interface Characteristics
docs.text:
• All methods implicitly public abstract
  • All fields implicitly public static final
  • Cannot have constructor
  • Cannot be instantiated
  • Can extend multiple interfaces
  • Example: 
    interface Drawable extends GetAreable {
  void draw();  // adds another abstract method
}

docs.title: Multiple Interface Implementation
docs.text:
• Class can implement many interfaces
  • Must implement all methods from all interfaces
  • Example: 
    class Square implements GetAreable, Comparable<Square> {
  @Override
  public double getArea() { return side * side; }

  @Override
  public int compareTo(Square other) {
    return Double.compare(this.getArea(), other.getArea());
  }
}

docs.title: Interface vs Abstract Class
docs.text: Feature Interface Abstract Class Multiple inheritance Yes No Method implementation Can have default methods Can have concrete methods Fields Only constants Any kind Constructor No Yes Access modifiers All public Any Purpose Define behavior Partial implementation Use case When multiple types may implement the same behavior When related classes share code

docs.title: Exception Handling Basics
docs.text:
• Mechanism to handle runtime errors
  • Uses try-catch-finally blocks
  • Allows separation of error handling from main logic
  • Example: 
    try {
  // code that might throw exception
  readFromFile(filename);
} catch (FileNotFoundException e) {
  // handle the exception
  System.err.println("File not found: " + e.getMessage());
} finally {
  // cleanup code, always executed
  closeFile();
}

docs.title: Types of Exceptions
docs.text:
1. Checked Exceptions:

    • Must be declared or caught
    • Extend Exception class
    • Example: IOException

  2. Unchecked Exceptions:

    • Don't need to be declared
    • Extend RuntimeException
    • Example: NullPointerException

  3. Errors:

    • Serious problems
    • Not meant to be caught
    • Example: OutOfMemoryError

docs.title: Creating Custom Exceptions
docs.text:
• Extend Exception or RuntimeException
  • Add constructors and fields as needed
  • Example: 
    class InvalidCircleException extends IllegalArgumentException {
  public InvalidCircleException(String message) {
    super(message);
  }
}

docs.title: Try-with-Resources
docs.text:
• Automatically closes resources
  • For classes implementing AutoCloseable
  • Example: 
    try (FileReader reader = new FileReader(file)) {
  // use reader
} catch (IOException e) {
  // handle exception
}
// reader automatically closed

docs.title: Wrapper Class Concept
docs.text:
• Classes that encapsulate primitive types
  • Convert primitives to objects
  • Provide utility methods
  • Enable use of primitives in generic code
  • Example: 
    Integer i = new Integer(42);  // wraps int
Double d = new Double(3.14);  // wraps double

docs.title: Java Wrapper Classes
docs.text: Primitive Wrapper byte Byte short Short int Integer long Long float Float double Double char Character boolean Boolean

docs.title: Auto-boxing and Unboxing
docs.text:
• Auto-boxing: Automatic primitive to wrapper conversion
  • Unboxing: Automatic wrapper to primitive conversion
  • Example: 
    Integer i = 42;    // auto-boxing
int n = i;         // unboxing

docs.title: Utility Methods
docs.text:
• Parsing methods
  • Value conversions
  • Comparisons
  • Example: 
    Integer.parseInt("123");     // String to int
Integer.toString(123);       // int to String
Integer.valueOf("123");      // String to Integer
Integer.compare(1, 2);       // compare two ints

docs.title: Run-Time Class Mismatch
docs.text:
• Occurs when actual type doesn't match expected type
  • Can lead to runtime errors
  • Example: 
    GetAreable[] shapes = new GetAreable[] {
  new Circle(p, 2),
  new Square(p, 5)
};
Circle c = (Circle) shapes[1];  // Runtime error: Square cannot be cast to Circle

docs.title: Safe Casting Practices
docs.text:
• Always check type before casting
  • Use instanceof operator
  • Example: 
    if (shape instanceof Circle) {
  Circle c = (Circle) shape;  // safe cast
  // use c
}

docs.title: Java Array Covariance
docs.text:
• Arrays are covariant in Java
  • Can lead to runtime errors
  • Example: 
    Integer[] intArray = new Integer[2];
Object[] objArray = intArray;        // OK due to covariance
objArray[0] = "Hello";              // Runtime error!

docs.title: Generic Types Concept
docs.text:
• Allow type parameters in class/interface definitions
  • Enable type-safe collections/operations
  • Prevent runtime type errors
  • Example: 
    class Box<T> {
  private T item;

  public void put(T item) {
    this.item = item;
  }

  public T get() {
    return item;
  }
}

docs.title: Type Parameters
docs.text:
• Commonly used letters:
    • T for type
    • E for element
    • K for key
    • V for value
    • S,U,V for multiple types
  • Example: 
    class Pair<K,V> {
  private K key;
  private V value;
}

docs.title: Bounded Type Parameters
docs.text:
• Restrict type parameters to certain types
  • Use extends keyword
  • Can bound to class or interface
  • Example: 
    class NumberBox<T extends Number> {
  private T number;

  public double getValue() {
    return number.doubleValue();  // OK because T extends Number
  }
}

docs.title: Type Erasure Concept
docs.text:
• Java's implementation of generics
  • Removes type parameters at compile time
  • Replaces with Object or bounds
  • Maintains backward compatibility
  • Example: 
    // Before erasure
class Box<T> {
  private T item;
}

// After erasure
class Box {
  private Object item;  // T replaced with Object
}

docs.title: Erasure with Bounds
docs.text:
• Bounded type replaced with bound
  • Example: 
    // Before erasure
class NumberBox<T extends Number> {
  private T number;
}

// After erasure
class NumberBox {
  private Number number;  // T replaced with Number
}

docs.title: Generic Method Type Inference
docs.text:
• Infers type arguments for generic methods
  • Based on:
    • Method arguments
    • Return type context
    • Type constraints
  • Example: 
    static <T> List<T> asList(T... elements) {
  return Arrays.asList(elements);
}

List<String> list = asList("a", "b", "c");  // T inferred as String

docs.title: Wildcard Types
docs.text:
• Represented by ?
  • Three types of wildcards:
    1. Upper Bounded: <? extends Type>
    2. Lower Bounded: <? super Type>
    3. Unbounded: <?>

docs.title: PECS Principle
docs.text:
• Producer Extends, Consumer Super
  • Use extends for input (reading)
  • Use super for output (writing)
  • Example: 
    // Producer (extends) - reading from source
void copyFromSource(List<? extends Shape> source) {
  Shape s = source.get(0);  // OK
}

// Consumer (super) - writing to dest
void copyToDest(List<? super Circle> dest) {
  dest.add(new Circle());  // OK
}

docs.title: Generic Restrictions and Best Practices
docs.text:
• Cannot create arrays of generic types
  • Cannot create generic exceptions
  • Cannot use primitives directly
  • Use wildcards appropriately
  • Document generic behavior
  • Consider type erasure implications

docs.title: Pure Functions
docs.text:
• No side effects
  • No printing to screen
  • No writing to files
  • No throwing exceptions
  • No changing other variables
  • Same input always gives same output
  • Example: 
    int square(int x) {
  return x * x;  // pure function
}

docs.title: First-Class Functions
docs.text:
• Functions as values
  • Can be passed as arguments
  • Can be returned from methods
  • Can be stored in variables
  • Example: 
    @FunctionalInterface
interface Transformer<T,R> {
  R transform(T t);
}

Transformer<Integer,Integer> square = x -> x * x;

docs.title: Lambda Expressions
docs.text:
• Anonymous function syntax
  • Concise method definitions
  • Used with functional interfaces
  • Example: 
    // Lambda syntax variations
x -> x + 1                    // single parameter
(x, y) -> x + y              // multiple parameters
(String s) -> s.length()     // with type
x -> { return x * x; }       // with block

docs.title: Method References
docs.text:
• Shorthand for lambdas
  • References existing methods
  • Four types:
    1. Static methods
    2. Instance methods
    3. Constructor references
    4. Arbitrary object methods
  • Example: 
    String::length              // instance method
System.out::println        // instance method
Integer::parseInt         // static method
ArrayList::new           // constructor

docs.title: Types of Nested Classes
docs.text:
1. Inner Classes
  2. Has access to all outer class members

  3. Requires instance of outer class

  4. Static Nested Classes

  5. Declared with static modifier

  6. Cannot access instance members of outer class

  7. Local Classes

  8. Defined inside methods

  9. Can access local variables (must be final/effectively final)

  10. Anonymous Classes

  11. Class definition and instantiation combined
  12. Example: 
    Runnable r = new Runnable() {
  @Override
  public void run() {
    System.out.println("Hello");
  }
};

docs.title:
InfiniteList
  • Represents potentially infinite sequences
  • Lazily evaluated
  • Uses head/tail structure
  • Example: 
    InfiniteList<Integer> numbers = InfiniteList
  .iterate(0, x -> x + 1)     // 0,1,2,3,...
  .filter(x -> x % 2 == 0)    // 0,2,4,6,...
  .map(x -> x * x)            // 0,4,16,36,...
  .limit(5);                  // first 5 elements

Monads
  • Container type with specific behavior rules
  • Supports sequential operations
  • Maintains computational context
  • Laws:
  • Left Identity
  • Right Identity
  • Associativity
Thread Concept
docs.text:
• Unit of program execution
  • Independent sequence of operations
  • Can run concurrently
  • Example: 
    Thread t = new Thread(() -> {
  System.out.println("Running in new thread");
});
t.start();

docs.title: Creating Threads
docs.text:
1. Using Runnable: 

    Runnable task = () -> {
  // task code here
};
Thread thread = new Thread(task);
thread.start();

  2. Extending Thread: 

    class MyThread extends Thread {
  public void run() {
    // task code here
  }
}
new MyThread().start();

docs.title: Thread States
docs.text:
• NEW: Created but not yet started
  • RUNNABLE: Executing or ready to execute
  • BLOCKED: Waiting for monitor lock
  • WAITING: Waiting indefinitely
  • TIMED_WAITING: Waiting for specified time
  • TERMINATED: Completed execution

docs.title: Thread Safety Issues
docs.text:
1. Race Conditions: 

    // Unsafe
counter++;  // not atomic

// Safe
synchronized(lock) {
  counter++;
}

  2. Visibility Problems: 

    // May have visibility issues
boolean flag = false;

// Guaranteed visibility
volatile boolean flag = false;

docs.title: Parallel Processing Concept
docs.text:
• Concurrent execution of tasks
  • Uses multiple threads/cores
  • Automatic work distribution
  • Example: 
    List<Integer> numbers = Arrays.asList(1, 2, 3, 4, 5);
numbers.parallelStream()
       .map(x -> x * 2)
       .collect(Collectors.toList());

docs.title: Creating Parallel Streams
docs.text:
1. From Collection: 

    // Method 1
list.parallelStream()

// Method 2
list.stream().parallel()

  2. From Arrays: 

    Arrays.stream(array).parallel()

docs.title: Performance Considerations
docs.text:
1. When to Use:

    • Large data sets
    • Computationally intensive
    • Independent operations
    • Multiple cores available

  2. When to Avoid:

    • Small data sets
    • IO-bound operations
    • Operations with dependencies
    • Single core systems

docs.title: CompletableFuture Concept
docs.text:
• Represents a future result
  • Supports non-blocking operations
  • Enables asynchronous programming
  • Example: 
    CompletableFuture<String> future = 
    CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> 
        expensiveOperation());

docs.title: Creating CompletableFuture
docs.text:
1. Static Factory Methods: 
    // Complete future
CompletableFuture.completedFuture(value);

// Run async task
CompletableFuture.runAsync(() -> task());

// Supply async result
CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> compute());

docs.title: Chaining Operations
docs.text:
1. Transformations: 

    future.thenApply(x -> x * 2)        // map
      .thenCompose(x -> compute(x))  // flatMap
      .thenCombine(other, (x,y) -> x + y);

  2. Side Effects: 

    future.thenAccept(System.out::println)  // consumer
      .thenRun(() -> cleanup());        // runnable

docs.title: Fork/Join Framework Concept
docs.text:
• Divide-and-conquer parallelism
  • Work-stealing algorithm
  • Recursive task decomposition
  • Example: 
    class SumTask extends RecursiveTask<Long> {
  private final long[] array;
  private final int start;
  private final int end;

  @Override
  protected Long compute() {
    if (end - start <= THRESHOLD) {
      return computeDirectly();
    }
    int mid = (start + end) / 2;
    SumTask left = new SumTask(array, start, mid);
    SumTask right = new SumTask(array, mid, end);
    left.fork();
    return right.compute() + left.join();
  }
}

docs.title: ForkJoinPool
docs.text:
• Thread pool for fork/join tasks
  • Work-stealing deque
  • Automatic load balancing
  • Example: 
    ForkJoinPool pool = new ForkJoinPool();
Long result = pool.invoke(new SumTask(array, 0, array.length));

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Choose appropriate threshold
  • Avoid synchronized methods
  • Minimize task dependencies
  • Handle exceptions properly
  • Test with different sizes

docs.title: Aliasing
docs.text:
• Multiple references pointing to the same object
  • Changes through one reference affect all references
  • Can lead to unexpected behavior
  • Example: 
    List<Integer> list1 = new ArrayList<>();
List<Integer> list2 = list1;  // alias created
list1.add(1);  // affects both list1 and list2

docs.title: Immutability Concept
docs.text:
• Objects cannot be modified after creation
  • All fields are final
  • No mutator methods
  • Returns new objects for modifications
  • Example: 
    final class Point {
  private final double x;
  private final double y;

  public Point(double x, double y) {
    this.x = x;
    this.y = y;
  }

  public Point moveTo(double newX, double newY) {
    return new Point(newX, newY);  // returns new instance
  }
}

docs.title: Benefits of Immutability
docs.text:
1. Thread Safety:

    • Safe for concurrent access
    • No synchronization needed

  2. Easier Reasoning:

    • No state changes to track
    • Predictable behavior

  3. Security:

    • Cannot be modified maliciously
    • Safe to share references

docs.title: Creating Immutable Classes
docs.text:
1. Make class final
  2. Make fields private and final
  3. No setter methods
  4. Return new instances for modifications
  5. Protect mutable fields

docs.title: Composition Concept
docs.text:
• Building complex classes using simpler classes
  • Models "HAS-A" relationship between classes
  • Example: 
    class Circle {
  private Point c;   // Circle HAS-A Point
  private double r;
}

docs.title: Benefits of Composition
docs.text:
• Builds layers of abstraction
  • Encapsulates implementation details
  • Makes code more modular
  • Allows reuse of existing classes
  • Simplifies complex class implementations

docs.title: Example of Composition
docs.text:
// Building up from Point to Circle to Cylinder
class Point {
  private double x;
  private double y;
}

class Circle {
  private Point center;
  private double radius;
}

class Cylinder {
  private Circle base;
  private double height;
}

docs.title: Reference Sharing Issues
docs.text:
• Objects can share references to components
  • Changes to shared components affect all objects
  • Solutions:
  • Avoid Sharing References
  • Create Defensive Copies

docs.title: Stream Concept
docs.text:
• Sequence of elements
  • Supports sequential/parallel operations
  • Lazy evaluation
  • Example: 
    Stream<String> stream = Stream.of("a", "b", "c");
List<String> result = stream
  .map(String::toUpperCase)
  .collect(Collectors.toList());

docs.title: Stream Operations
docs.text:
1. Intermediate Operations:

    • map: Transform elements
    • filter: Select elements
    • flatMap: One-to-many transformation
    • sorted: Sort elements
    • distinct: Remove duplicates

  2. Terminal Operations:

    • collect: Accumulate elements
    • forEach: Process elements
    • reduce: Combine elements
    • count: Count elements

docs.title: Stream Characteristics
docs.text:
• Single use only
  • Lazy evaluation
  • Non-interference required
  • Stateless preferred
  • Example: 
    Stream<String> stream = list.stream();
stream.forEach(System.out::println);
stream.forEach(System.out::println);  // IllegalStateException
